result:
**NON-BINDING TERM SHEET --- TECHNOLOGY LICENSE AGREEMENT**

**PredictIQ Platform License and Related Services**

May 29, 2025

**CONFIDENTIAL**

Prepared by Bergström Hecht Rechtsanwälte, Maximilianstraße 42, 80538 Munich, Germany, on behalf of Kessler-Brandt Industrial GmbH. Lead Partner: Dr. Luisa Trautmann.

This term sheet is intended to summarize the principal terms of a proposed technology license arrangement between Kessler-Brandt Industrial GmbH and Whitmore Analytics Inc. This term sheet is non-binding and does not constitute an offer or agreement. The parties intend to negotiate and execute a definitive agreement based on these terms.

**Targeted Definitive Agreement Execution:** August 15, 2025

**Requested Response Deadline:** KBI respectfully requests that Whitmore provide a written response to this term sheet on or before **June 23, 2025**.

**[Section 1: Transaction Overview]{.underline}**

**Licensor:** Whitmore Analytics Inc., a Delaware corporation (\"**Whitmore**\"), with principal offices at 2400 Westlake Avenue North, Suite 600, Seattle, WA 98109. CEO: Rajesh Malhotra. General Counsel: Sarah Yin.

**Licensee:** Kessler-Brandt Industrial GmbH, a Gesellschaft mit beschränkter Haftung organized under the laws of Germany, registered in Munich (HRB 247891) (\"**KBI**\"), with principal offices at Leopoldstraße 155, 80804 Munich, Germany. CTO: Dr. Markus Fiedler. VP of Digital Transformation: Annika Solberg.

**Licensee\'s Counsel:** Bergström Hecht Rechtsanwälte, Maximilianstraße 42, 80538 Munich, Germany. Lead Partner: Dr. Luisa Trautmann.

**Licensed Technology:** Whitmore\'s proprietary PredictIQ platform, a cloud-native SaaS platform combining IoT sensor integration with proprietary machine-learning algorithms to predict equipment failures in heavy manufacturing environments, as more fully described in **Exhibit A** attached hereto.

**Purpose:** KBI seeks to integrate PredictIQ into its \"SmartFactory 2030\" initiative to reduce unplanned downtime by 40% across its European manufacturing operations.

**Transaction Structure:** The proposed transaction contemplates: (i) a perpetual, non-exclusive technology license for the PredictIQ platform; (ii) an initial 18-month implementation phase followed by ongoing production use; (iii) a SaaS subscription component for the cloud-hosted analytics engine; and (iv) an on-premises deployment license for edge-computing modules installed at KBI factory sites.

**Deployment Scope:** KBI shall have the right to deploy PredictIQ at up to 43 manufacturing facilities across 12 European countries. KBI shall have the right to expand deployment to any newly acquired manufacturing facilities under the same commercial terms for a period of 5 years following execution of the definitive agreement.

**Key Milestones:**

> • Phase 1 implementation start: September 15, 2025
>
> • Phase 1 completion target: March 15, 2026 (6 months)
>
> • Phase 2 go-live target: March 15, 2027 (18 months from implementation start)

**Whitmore Background:** Whitmore holds 14 issued U.S. utility patents and 3 pending PCT applications related to PredictIQ\'s core algorithms.

**KBI Background:** KBI is a global industrial conglomerate with €8.2B annual revenue, operating 43 manufacturing facilities across 12 European countries.

**[Section 2: License Grant]{.underline}**

**Grant.** Whitmore hereby grants to KBI a perpetual, non-exclusive, worldwide license to use, execute, display, and operate the PredictIQ platform (including all software, algorithms, documentation, APIs, and edge-computing modules) in connection with KBI\'s manufacturing operations.

**Scope.** The license covers both the cloud-hosted SaaS analytics engine and the on-premises edge-computing modules deployed at KBI\'s manufacturing facilities.

**Authorized Users.** KBI employees, contractors, and agents performing work at or for KBI\'s manufacturing facilities shall be authorized to access and use the PredictIQ platform, subject to confidentiality obligations no less restrictive than those set forth in the definitive agreement.

**Sublicensing.** KBI shall not sublicense the PredictIQ platform to third parties, except that KBI may permit its controlled affiliates and subcontractors performing services at KBI facilities to access and use PredictIQ in support of KBI\'s operations.

**Derivative Works.** KBI shall have the right to create derivative works based on PredictIQ\'s application programming interface (API) layer for the sole purpose of integrating the PredictIQ platform with KBI\'s proprietary manufacturing execution systems (MES), supervisory control and data acquisition (SCADA) systems, and other internal systems. Any and all derivative works created by or on behalf of KBI pursuant to this Section shall be jointly owned by KBI and Whitmore, with each party having the unrestricted right to use, modify, sublicense, and otherwise exploit such derivative works without the consent of, or any obligation to account to, the other party.

**Expansion Right.** For a period of 5 years following execution of the definitive agreement, KBI shall have the right to deploy PredictIQ at any manufacturing facility acquired by KBI after the date of execution, at the same per-facility commercial terms as set forth herein.

**Restrictions.** KBI shall not: (i) reverse engineer, decompile, or disassemble any portion of PredictIQ, except as expressly permitted by applicable law; (ii) use PredictIQ for the benefit of any third party other than as expressly permitted herein; or (iii) remove or alter any proprietary notices on the PredictIQ platform.

**[Section 3: Intellectual Property Ownership]{.underline}**

**3.1 Whitmore IP.** As between the parties, Whitmore retains all right, title, and interest in and to the PredictIQ platform, including all software, algorithms, machine-learning models, source code, object code, documentation, patents (14 issued U.S. utility patents and 3 pending PCT applications), trade secrets, and all other intellectual property rights therein. Nothing in this term sheet shall be construed to transfer ownership of PredictIQ or any underlying intellectual property from Whitmore to KBI, except as expressly set forth herein.

**3.2 KBI IP.** KBI retains all right, title, and interest in and to its proprietary systems, data, processes, and know-how, including its MES, SCADA systems, and all operational data.

**3.3 Derivative Works.** As set forth in Section 2, any derivative works created by KBI from PredictIQ\'s API layer shall be jointly owned by KBI and Whitmore, with each party having the unrestricted right to use, modify, sublicense, and otherwise exploit such derivative works without the consent of, or any obligation to account to, the other party.

**3.4 Feedback.** Any suggestions, ideas, enhancement requests, recommendations, improvements, or other feedback communicated by KBI or its personnel to Whitmore regarding PredictIQ or its functionality (\"**Feedback**\") shall become the sole and exclusive property of Whitmore. KBI hereby assigns to Whitmore all right, title, and interest in and to any such Feedback, and Whitmore shall be free to use, incorporate, and exploit such Feedback in any manner without restriction or obligation to KBI.

**3.5 Improvements Derived from KBI Data.** Notwithstanding Section 3.4, to the extent that Whitmore develops any improvements, enhancements, or modifications to the PredictIQ platform that are derived from or inspired by KBI\'s operational data, manufacturing processes, or use of the PredictIQ platform (\"**KBI-Inspired Improvements**\"), KBI shall be granted a perpetual, irrevocable, royalty-free, non-exclusive license to use such KBI-Inspired Improvements within its manufacturing operations. For clarity, the foregoing license shall apply regardless of whether such improvements were developed in response to Feedback provided by KBI.

**[Section 4: Data Rights]{.underline}**

**4.1 KBI Data.** \"**KBI Data**\" means all data, information, and content provided by KBI or generated by KBI\'s systems, equipment, or sensors that is input into or processed by the PredictIQ platform, including but not limited to operational data, sensor readings, equipment telemetry, maintenance records, production schedules, and environmental data. KBI retains all right, title, and interest in and to KBI Data.

**4.2 Output Data.** \"**Output Data**\" means all data, analytics, results, reports, predictions, alerts, and other outputs generated by the PredictIQ platform when processing KBI Data, including, without limitation, model weights, parameters, training artifacts, feature importance rankings, and model configuration data derived from KBI Data. All Output Data shall be the sole and exclusive property of KBI.

**4.3 Restrictions on Use of KBI Data.** Whitmore shall not use KBI Data, or any data derived therefrom, for any purpose other than performing its obligations under the definitive agreement, including without limitation: (a) training, tuning, or improving PredictIQ\'s general-purpose machine-learning models; (b) developing or improving any products or services for the benefit of any third party; or (c) creating anonymized, aggregated, or de-identified datasets from KBI Data. For the avoidance of doubt, Whitmore shall not use KBI Data in anonymized or aggregated form to train or improve PredictIQ\'s general models without KBI\'s prior written consent, which may be withheld in KBI\'s sole discretion.

**4.4 Return and Deletion.** Upon termination or expiration of the definitive agreement, Whitmore shall, at KBI\'s election, return or securely delete all KBI Data and Output Data in its possession, and certify such return or deletion in writing.

**4.5 Compliance.** Both parties shall comply with all applicable data protection laws, including but not limited to the EU General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) and applicable German federal and state data protection laws.

**[Section 5: Fees and Payment]{.underline}**

**5.1 Initial License Fee.** \$4,200,000 (Four Million Two Hundred Thousand U.S. Dollars), payable in three installments:

> \(a\) \$1,260,000 (30%) due upon execution of the definitive agreement;
>
> \(b\) \$1,680,000 (40%) due upon completion of Phase 1 implementation (targeted: March 15, 2026);
>
> \(c\) \$1,260,000 (30%) due upon go-live of Phase 2 (targeted: March 15, 2027).

**5.2 Annual SaaS Subscription Fee.** \$2,850,000 per year for Years 1 through 3, payable quarterly in advance (\$712,500 per quarter). Beginning Year 4, the annual SaaS Subscription Fee shall escalate by 3% per annum. Year 4 fee: \$2,935,500. Year 5 fee: \$3,023,565.

**5.3 Implementation Services Fee.** \$1,750,000 (One Million Seven Hundred Fifty Thousand U.S. Dollars), covering on-site deployment, integration with KBI\'s existing SCADA and MES systems, data migration, and end-user training. Payable in equal monthly installments over the 18-month implementation period (\$97,222.22 per month).

**5.4 Annual Maintenance and Support Fee.** 18% of the Initial License Fee per annum = \$756,000 per year (Year 1). The Annual Maintenance and Support Fee shall escalate by 4% per annum beginning Year 2. Year 2: \$786,240. Year 3: \$817,689.60. Payable annually in advance.

**5.5 Currency and Payment Terms.** All fees are payable in U.S. Dollars. Payment due within 30 days of invoice. Late payments shall accrue interest at the rate of 1.5% per month or the maximum rate permitted by applicable law, whichever is less.

**5.6 Taxes.** All fees are exclusive of applicable taxes. KBI shall be responsible for all sales, use, value-added, and similar taxes (excluding Whitmore\'s income taxes).

**5.7 Expansion Pricing.** Deployment to newly acquired KBI facilities during the 5-year expansion period shall be at the same per-facility commercial terms as set forth herein, without any additional license fee, calculated pro rata based on 43 facilities.

**[Section 6: Implementation Services]{.underline}**

**6.1 Scope.** Whitmore shall provide professional services to implement the PredictIQ platform at KBI\'s 43 manufacturing facilities across 12 European countries, including:

> \(a\) On-site deployment of edge-computing modules at each facility;
>
> \(b\) Integration with KBI\'s existing SCADA and MES systems;
>
> \(c\) Data ingestion pipeline configuration for IoT sensor data;
>
> \(d\) Model calibration and initial training using KBI\'s historical operational data;
>
> \(e\) End-user training for KBI\'s operations and maintenance personnel;
>
> \(f\) User acceptance testing at each facility.

**6.2 Phases.**

> *Phase 1* (Months 1--6): Deployment at 10 pilot facilities. Start: September 15, 2025. Completion target: March 15, 2026.
>
> *Phase 2* (Months 7--18): Deployment at remaining 33 facilities. Completion target: March 15, 2027.

**6.3 KBI Responsibilities.** KBI shall provide reasonable access to its facilities, systems, data, and personnel. KBI shall designate a project manager to serve as the primary point of contact. KBI shall ensure its SCADA and MES systems are operational and meet Whitmore\'s published minimum system requirements.

**6.4 Change Orders.** Any changes to the implementation scope shall be documented in written change orders signed by both parties, with associated fees agreed upon before work commences.

**6.5 Acceptance.** Each facility deployment shall be subject to a user acceptance test (UAT) period of 30 days. Acceptance shall be deemed granted if KBI does not provide written notice of material non-conformance within the UAT period.

**[Section 7: Maintenance and Support]{.underline}**

**7.1 Scope.** Whitmore shall provide maintenance and support services for the PredictIQ platform, including: (a) bug fixes and error corrections; (b) software updates and patches; (c) 24/7 technical support via phone, email, and online portal; and (d) annual platform upgrades (major releases).

**7.2 Service Levels.** Whitmore shall use commercially reasonable efforts to achieve the following service level targets:

  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Severity**             **Response Time**       **Resolution Target**
  ------------------------ ----------------------- ---------------------------
  Critical (system down)   4 hours                 24 hours

  High                     8 hours                 72 hours

  Medium / Low             2 business days         As reasonably practicable
  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------

**7.3 Fee.** As set forth in Section 5.4 (\$756,000 per year, escalating 4% annually).

**7.4 Term.** Maintenance and support shall commence upon Phase 1 go-live and continue for the duration of the license unless terminated in accordance with the definitive agreement.

**7.5 Suspension.** Whitmore reserves the right to suspend maintenance and support in the event KBI fails to pay applicable maintenance fees when due, subject to 30 days\' written notice to KBI.

**[Section 8: Warranties]{.underline}**

**8.1 Conformance Warranty.** Whitmore warrants that the PredictIQ platform shall perform materially in accordance with the Documentation provided to KBI for a period of 24 months from the applicable go-live date at each facility.

**8.2 Performance Warranty.** Whitmore warrants that the PredictIQ platform shall achieve a prediction accuracy rate of no less than 92% as measured on a rolling 90-day basis across all deployed KBI facilities. \"Prediction accuracy\" shall be defined as the percentage of equipment failure events correctly predicted by PredictIQ at least 48 hours in advance of occurrence, measured against actual failure events recorded in KBI\'s maintenance logs.

**8.3 Performance Remedies.**

> \(a\) For each percentage point by which the rolling 90-day prediction accuracy falls below 92%, KBI shall receive a credit equal to 5% of the quarterly SaaS Subscription Fee applicable to the relevant quarter (quarterly SaaS Fee: \$712,500; credit per percentage point below 92%: \$35,625).
>
> \(b\) If the prediction accuracy rate falls below 85% for two consecutive calendar quarters, KBI shall have the right to terminate the definitive agreement for cause pursuant to Section 13.

**8.4 IP Warranty.** Whitmore represents and warrants that: (a) it is the owner or authorized licensee of all intellectual property rights in PredictIQ; (b) PredictIQ does not infringe any third-party intellectual property rights to Whitmore\'s knowledge; and (c) Whitmore has full right and authority to grant the licenses contemplated herein.

**8.5 Disclaimer.** EXCEPT AS EXPRESSLY SET FORTH IN THIS SECTION 8, WHITMORE MAKES NO WARRANTIES, EXPRESS OR IMPLIED, INCLUDING ANY IMPLIED WARRANTIES OF MERCHANTABILITY, FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE, OR NON-INFRINGEMENT.

**[Section 9: Indemnification]{.underline}**

**9.1 IP Indemnification by Whitmore.** Whitmore shall defend, indemnify, and hold harmless KBI, its affiliates, officers, directors, employees, and agents from and against any and all claims, actions, suits, damages, losses, costs, and expenses (including reasonable attorneys\' fees) arising from any third-party claim alleging that KBI\'s authorized use of the PredictIQ platform infringes or misappropriates any patent, copyright, trade secret, or other intellectual property right of any third party. Whitmore\'s obligations under this Section 9.1 shall not be subject to any limitation of liability or cap on damages.

**9.2 Infringement Remedies.** If PredictIQ is found to infringe or Whitmore believes it is likely to be found to infringe, Whitmore shall, at its sole option and expense: (a) procure for KBI the right to continue using PredictIQ; (b) modify PredictIQ to make it non-infringing while maintaining substantially equivalent functionality; or (c) replace PredictIQ with a non-infringing alternative of substantially equivalent functionality.

**9.3 KBI Indemnification.** KBI shall defend, indemnify, and hold harmless Whitmore from and against any third-party claims arising from: (a) KBI\'s use of PredictIQ in a manner not authorized by the definitive agreement; or (b) KBI Data or KBI\'s proprietary systems, to the extent such claims do not arise from PredictIQ itself.

**9.4 Indemnification Procedures.** The indemnified party shall: (a) promptly notify the indemnifying party of any claim; (b) grant the indemnifying party sole control of the defense and settlement; and (c) provide reasonable cooperation at the indemnifying party\'s expense.

**[Section 10: Limitation of Liability]{.underline}**

**10.1 General Cap.** EXCEPT AS SET FORTH IN SECTION 10.3, NEITHER PARTY\'S AGGREGATE LIABILITY UNDER THE DEFINITIVE AGREEMENT SHALL EXCEED THE GREATER OF: (I) TWO TIMES (2×) THE TOTAL FEES PAID OR PAYABLE BY KBI IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE EVENT GIVING RISE TO THE CLAIM, OR (II) FIFTEEN MILLION U.S. DOLLARS (\$15,000,000).

**10.2 Exclusion of Consequential Damages.** EXCEPT AS SET FORTH IN SECTION 10.3, NEITHER PARTY SHALL BE LIABLE TO THE OTHER FOR ANY INDIRECT, INCIDENTAL, SPECIAL, CONSEQUENTIAL, OR PUNITIVE DAMAGES, INCLUDING LOST PROFITS, LOSS OF DATA, LOSS OF GOODWILL, OR BUSINESS INTERRUPTION, REGARDLESS OF THE THEORY OF LIABILITY.

**10.3 Exceptions.** THE LIMITATIONS SET FORTH IN SECTIONS 10.1 AND 10.2 SHALL NOT APPLY TO: (A) WHITMORE\'S INDEMNIFICATION OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 9.1 (IP INDEMNIFICATION); (B) EITHER PARTY\'S BREACH OF ITS CONFIDENTIALITY OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 15; OR (C) EITHER PARTY\'S LIABILITY FOR WILLFUL MISCONDUCT OR FRAUD.

**[Section 11: Source Code Escrow]{.underline}**

**11.1 Deposit.** Within 60 days of execution of the definitive agreement, Whitmore shall deposit with Meridian Escrow Services LLC, based in San Jose, California (the \"**Escrow Agent**\"), the following materials: (a) complete source code for the PredictIQ platform (including all modules, libraries, and dependencies); (b) all build tools, compilers, and scripts necessary to compile and deploy the source code; (c) complete technical documentation, including architecture diagrams, database schemas, and API specifications; and (d) updated materials upon each major release or material update. Whitmore shall update the escrow deposit at least annually and upon each major release.

**11.2 Release Conditions.** The Escrow Agent shall release the escrowed materials to KBI upon the occurrence of any of the following events:

> \(a\) Whitmore\'s insolvency or filing for bankruptcy protection under applicable law;
>
> \(b\) Whitmore\'s material breach of the definitive agreement that remains uncured for a period of sixty (60) days following written notice from KBI;
>
> \(c\) Whitmore\'s cessation of business operations;
>
> \(d\) Whitmore\'s failure to provide maintenance and support services for the PredictIQ platform for a period of ninety (90) consecutive days.

**11.3 Post-Release License.** Upon release of the escrowed materials pursuant to Section 11.2, KBI shall be granted a perpetual, irrevocable, fully paid-up, royalty-free license to use, modify, compile, and deploy the source code and related materials solely for KBI\'s internal manufacturing operations.

**11.4 Escrow Fees.** Escrow agent fees shall be borne equally by the parties.

**11.5 Verification.** KBI shall have the right, no more than once per calendar year, to request that the Escrow Agent verify the completeness and usability of the escrowed materials.

**[Section 12: Exclusivity / Non-Compete]{.underline}**

**12.1 Exclusivity Period.** During the period commencing on the effective date of the definitive agreement and ending on the third (3rd) anniversary thereof (the \"**Exclusivity Period**\"), Whitmore shall not license, sublicense, sell, or otherwise make available the PredictIQ platform (or any substantially similar predictive maintenance technology) to any Direct Competitor (as defined below) of KBI in the European automotive and heavy machinery manufacturing sectors.

**12.2 Definition of Direct Competitor.** \"**Direct Competitor**\" means: (a) each of the entities listed in **Exhibit B** to this term sheet; and (b) any entity that derives more than thirty percent (30%) of its annual revenue from the manufacture of automotive vehicles, automotive components, heavy machinery, or heavy industrial equipment in Europe (defined for this purpose as the European Economic Area plus the United Kingdom and Switzerland).

**12.3 Scope.** The exclusivity restriction shall apply to the PredictIQ platform and any successor or replacement product offering substantially similar predictive maintenance functionality for manufacturing operations.

**12.4 Survival.** The exclusivity obligation shall survive termination of the definitive agreement for any reason and shall continue for the remainder of the Exclusivity Period.

**12.5 Remedies.** In the event of a breach of this Section 12, KBI shall be entitled to injunctive relief in addition to any other remedies available at law or in equity. KBI shall not be required to demonstrate irreparable harm or post a bond as a condition to obtaining injunctive relief.

**[Section 13: Term and Termination]{.underline}**

**13.1 Term.** The definitive agreement shall have a perpetual term, subject to termination as set forth in this Section 13.

**13.2 Termination for Cause.** Either party may terminate the definitive agreement upon ninety (90) days\' written notice to the other party in the event of a material breach by such other party, provided that the breaching party fails to cure such breach within sixty (60) days following receipt of written notice specifying the nature of the breach in reasonable detail.

**13.3 Termination for Convenience by KBI.** KBI may terminate the definitive agreement for convenience upon twelve (12) months\' prior written notice, provided such notice is given no earlier than the third (3rd) anniversary of the effective date.

**13.4 Termination for Performance Failure.** KBI may terminate the definitive agreement for cause if the prediction accuracy rate (as defined in Section 8.2) falls below 85% for two (2) consecutive calendar quarters, upon 30 days\' written notice.

**13.5 Wind-Down License.** Upon termination or expiration of the definitive agreement for any reason (including termination by KBI for convenience under Section 13.3), KBI shall retain a perpetual, non-exclusive, irrevocable license to continue using the version of the PredictIQ platform deployed at KBI\'s facilities as of the effective date of termination (the \"**Wind-Down License**\"). The Wind-Down License shall be subject to KBI\'s continued payment of the Annual Maintenance and Support Fee as set forth in Section 5.4.

**13.6 Effects of Termination.** Upon termination: (a) KBI\'s right to new SaaS features, updates, and new deployments shall cease; (b) all outstanding fees accrued through the effective date of termination shall become immediately due and payable; (c) each party shall return or destroy the other party\'s confidential information, subject to the Wind-Down License; and (d) the provisions of Sections 3, 4, 9, 10, 11, 12, 15, and 16 shall survive termination.

**13.7 No Refunds.** Fees paid prior to termination are non-refundable.

**[Section 14: Governing Law and Dispute Resolution]{.underline}**

**14.1 Governing Law.** The definitive agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the substantive laws of the Federal Republic of Germany, without regard to conflicts of law principles.

**14.2 Arbitration.** Any dispute, controversy, or claim arising out of or in connection with the definitive agreement, or the breach, termination, or invalidity thereof, shall be finally settled by arbitration administered by the International Chamber of Commerce (ICC) in accordance with the ICC Rules of Arbitration in effect at the time of filing. The arbitral tribunal shall consist of three (3) arbitrators. The seat of arbitration shall be Zurich, Switzerland. The language of the arbitral proceedings shall be English.

**14.3 Interim Measures.** Nothing in this Section 14 shall prevent either party from seeking interim or injunctive relief from any court of competent jurisdiction in connection with matters relating to intellectual property rights, confidentiality, or the preservation of the status quo pending arbitration.

**14.4 Costs.** Each party shall bear its own costs and legal fees, and the costs of arbitration (including arbitrator fees) shall be allocated by the tribunal.

**[Section 15: Confidentiality]{.underline}**

**15.1 Confidential Information.** Each party shall maintain in confidence all non-public information received from the other party in connection with this term sheet and the definitive agreement (\"**Confidential Information**\").

**15.2 Exceptions.** The foregoing confidentiality obligations shall not apply to information that: (a) is or becomes publicly available through no fault of the receiving party; (b) was rightfully known to the receiving party prior to disclosure; (c) was independently developed by the receiving party without use of or reference to the disclosing party\'s Confidential Information; or (d) was rightfully received from a third party without restriction on disclosure.

**15.3 Permitted Disclosures.** Either party may disclose Confidential Information to its advisors, accountants, and legal counsel who are bound by confidentiality obligations, and as required by law or regulation.

**15.4 Term.** Confidentiality obligations shall survive termination of the definitive agreement for a period of 5 years.

**15.5 Return.** Upon termination, each party shall return or destroy the other party\'s Confidential Information, subject to archival copies retained pursuant to legal or regulatory requirements and the Wind-Down License.

**[Section 16: Miscellaneous]{.underline}**

**16.1 Non-Binding.** This term sheet is non-binding and does not create any obligation on either party to enter into a definitive agreement. Notwithstanding the foregoing, Sections 15 (Confidentiality) and 16 (Miscellaneous) shall be binding upon the parties.

**16.2 Exclusivity of Negotiations.** For a period of 60 days from the date of this term sheet (i.e., through July 28, 2025), Whitmore shall negotiate exclusively with KBI and shall not solicit, entertain, or accept any proposals from third parties for a comparable technology licensing transaction for the European industrial market.

**16.3 Entire Understanding.** This term sheet constitutes the entire understanding between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof, together with the side letter regarding most-favored-customer pricing dated May 29, 2025.

**16.4 Expenses.** Each party shall bear its own expenses incurred in connection with the negotiation and execution of the definitive agreement.

**16.5 Notices.** All notices shall be in writing and delivered to the addresses set forth in Section 1.

**16.6 Assignment.** Neither party may assign its rights or obligations without the prior written consent of the other party, except that KBI may assign to any affiliate or in connection with a merger, acquisition, or sale of all or substantially all of its assets.

**16.7 Response Deadline.** KBI requests that Whitmore provide a written response to this term sheet on or before June 23, 2025.

**SIGNATURE PAGE**

ACKNOWLEDGED AND AGREED as to the binding provisions of Sections 15 and 16 only:

**For Kessler-Brandt Industrial GmbH:**

KESSLER-BRANDT INDUSTRIAL GmbH

By: **\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_**

Name: Dr. Markus Fiedler

Title: Chief Technology Officer

Date: May 29, 2025

By: **\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_**

Name: Annika Solberg

Title: VP of Digital Transformation

Date: May 29, 2025

**For Whitmore Analytics Inc.:**

WHITMORE ANALYTICS INC.

By: **\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_**

Name: Rajesh Malhotra

Title: Chief Executive Officer

Date: **\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_**

By: **\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_**

Name: Sarah Yin

Title: General Counsel

Date: **\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_**

**EXHIBIT A**

**Description of PredictIQ Platform**

**Product Name:** PredictIQ

**Description:** Cloud-native SaaS platform combining IoT sensor integration with proprietary machine-learning algorithms for predictive maintenance in heavy manufacturing environments.

**Core Components:**

> \(a\) *Cloud-hosted analytics engine (SaaS):* Real-time data processing, ML model inference, dashboarding, alerting, and reporting.
>
> \(b\) *Edge-computing modules (on-premises):* Deployed at individual factory sites for local data collection, preprocessing, and low-latency inference.
>
> \(c\) *API layer:* RESTful APIs enabling integration with third-party MES, SCADA, ERP, and other industrial systems.
>
> \(d\) *IoT integration layer:* Protocols supporting OPC UA, MQTT, Modbus, and proprietary sensor interfaces.

**ML Capabilities:** Proprietary algorithms for equipment failure prediction, anomaly detection, remaining useful life estimation, and maintenance scheduling optimization.

**Protected IP:** 14 issued U.S. utility patents covering core algorithms; 3 pending PCT applications.

**Documentation:** Administrator guide, user guide, API reference, integration guide, and model configuration reference.

**System Requirements:** Minimum hardware and software requirements for edge modules and network connectivity specifications shall be provided by Whitmore in a separate technical specification document prior to commencement of Phase 1 implementation.

**EXHIBIT B**

**Direct Competitors of Kessler-Brandt Industrial GmbH --- Exclusivity Restriction (Section 12)**

The following entities are designated as Direct Competitors for purposes of Section 12:

1\. Voelker-Haas Maschinenbau AG (Germany)

2\. Duraforge Industries S.p.A. (Italy)

3\. Nordhaven Automotive Group AB (Sweden)

4\. Castellain Manufacturing S.A. (France)

5\. Ironbridge Heavy Industries plc (United Kingdom)

6\. Rhenania Precision Components GmbH (Germany)

7\. Balticore Systems Oy (Finland)

8\. Vandermeer Werktuigbouw B.V. (Netherlands)

9\. Erhardt Industrietechnik AG (Austria)

10\. Solvena Motors S.L. (Spain)

11\. Krakowski Maszynowy S.A. (Poland)

12\. Helvetian Drivetrain AG (Switzerland)

In addition to the entities listed above, the term \"Direct Competitor\" includes any entity deriving more than 30% of its annual revenue from the manufacture of automotive vehicles, automotive components, heavy machinery, or heavy industrial equipment in the European Economic Area, the United Kingdom, or Switzerland, as set forth in Section 12.2(b).

result:
**KESSLER-BRANDT INDUSTRIAL GmbH**

Leopoldstraße 155 · 80804 Munich · Germany Handelsregister: HRB 247891 (Amtsgericht München)

*Office of the Vice President, Digital Transformation*

May 29, 2025

Sarah Yin General Counsel Whitmore Analytics Inc. 2400 Westlake Avenue North, Suite 600 Seattle, WA 98109

**Re: Most-Favored-Customer Pricing Terms --- Proposed Technology License Agreement between Kessler-Brandt Industrial GmbH and Whitmore Analytics Inc. (the \"PredictIQ License\")**

Dear Ms. Yin:

**[1. Introduction and Context]{.underline}**

This side letter is supplemental to, and is intended to be incorporated into, the definitive Technology License Agreement to be entered into between Kessler-Brandt Industrial GmbH (\"KBI\") and Whitmore Analytics Inc. (\"Whitmore\") with respect to the PredictIQ platform. Reference is hereby made to the non-binding term sheet dated May 29, 2025, prepared by KBI\'s counsel, Bergström Hecht Rechtsanwälte (Dr. Luisa Trautmann), setting forth the proposed commercial and legal terms for KBI\'s license of the PredictIQ platform (the \"Term Sheet\"). KBI\'s willingness to proceed with the transaction on the commercial terms outlined in the Term Sheet is **expressly conditioned** upon Whitmore\'s acceptance of the most-favored-customer pricing provision set forth in Section 2 below.

KBI has committed to a significant financial investment in this engagement: an Initial License Fee of \$4,200,000, an Annual SaaS Subscription Fee of \$2,850,000 per year, an Implementation Services Fee of \$1,750,000, and an Annual Maintenance Fee of \$756,000 --- representing total first-year revenue to Whitmore of approximately \$9,556,000. Under KBI\'s SmartFactory 2030 initiative, the PredictIQ platform is planned for deployment across 43 manufacturing facilities in 12 European countries. Given the scale and strategic importance of this engagement, KBI considers pricing parity assurance to be a fundamental commercial term.

**[2. Most-Favored-Customer Pricing Provision]{.underline}**

**(a) MFC Commitment.** If at any time during the term of the Agreement (or thereafter, for so long as KBI retains any license rights under the Agreement, including any post-termination wind-down license), Whitmore enters into any agreement, amendment, or arrangement with any third party (a \"Subsequent Licensee\") for the license, subscription, or use of the PredictIQ platform or any successor, derivative, or substantially similar technology, on terms that result in a lower effective per-facility price than the effective per-facility price applicable to KBI under the Agreement, Whitmore shall promptly (and in any event within thirty (30) days of execution of such agreement) notify KBI in writing and retroactively adjust KBI\'s pricing to match the more favorable effective per-facility pricing, effective as of the date such more favorable terms were first offered to the Subsequent Licensee.

**(b) Effective Per-Facility Price Calculation.** \"Effective per-facility price\" shall mean the total annual fees payable by the applicable licensee (including, without limitation, license fees, subscription fees, maintenance and support fees, and implementation or professional services fees, whether characterized as one-time or recurring), divided by the number of facilities at which the PredictIQ platform is deployed or authorized for deployment.

**(c) Retroactive Adjustment and Credit.** Any reduction in KBI\'s effective per-facility price shall be applied retroactively to the date on which Whitmore first offered or agreed to the more favorable terms with the Subsequent Licensee. Whitmore shall issue a credit to KBI equal to the aggregate overpayment for the period from such retroactive date through the date of adjustment, such credit to be applied against KBI\'s next payment(s) due under the Agreement.

**(d) Audit Right.** KBI shall have the right, upon reasonable notice and no more than once per calendar year, to engage an independent auditor (at KBI\'s expense, unless the audit reveals a discrepancy of five percent (5%) or more, in which case at Whitmore\'s expense) to verify Whitmore\'s compliance with this provision. Whitmore shall maintain complete and accurate records of all PredictIQ licensing arrangements sufficient to enable such verification.

**[3. Rationale and Importance to KBI]{.underline}**

As an early and significant adopter of PredictIQ --- committing to deployment across its entire European manufacturing footprint --- KBI expects pricing that reflects the value of this anchor-customer relationship. The effective per-facility pricing metric ensures fair comparison across licensees of different scale. Most-favored-customer protections of this nature are standard commercial practice for transactions of this magnitude; KBI, with annual revenue of €8.2 billion and a position as a leading European industrial conglomerate, routinely includes such provisions in its strategic technology engagements. KBI believes this provision aligns the parties\' long-term commercial interests and fosters an enduring partnership.

**[4. Incorporation into Definitive Agreement]{.underline}**

KBI expects the most-favored-customer pricing provision set forth in Section 2 above to be incorporated verbatim (or in substantially similar form) into the definitive Technology License Agreement targeted for execution on or about August 15, 2025. KBI respectfully requests Whitmore\'s written response to this side letter no later than **June 23, 2025**. KBI\'s willingness to adhere to the proposed transaction timeline --- including the September 15, 2025 Phase 1 implementation start date --- is contingent upon Whitmore\'s acceptance of this provision by such date. KBI acknowledges that this side letter is non-binding in itself but constitutes a material element of KBI\'s overall commercial proposal.

**[5. Confidentiality]{.underline}**

This side letter and its contents are confidential and subject to the confidentiality provisions of the Term Sheet. Neither party shall disclose the existence or terms of this side letter to any third party without the other party\'s prior written consent, except to their respective professional advisors (including, in the case of Whitmore, legal counsel such as Fenwick Hale & Seward LLP) under obligations of confidentiality.

**[6. Governing Law]{.underline}**

This side letter shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of Germany, consistent with the governing law provision of the Term Sheet.

We are genuinely enthusiastic about the partnership between KBI and Whitmore and the transformative potential of the PredictIQ platform within our SmartFactory 2030 initiative. However, the most-favored-customer pricing provision described above is a fundamental commercial term without which KBI would need to reassess the overall economics of the proposed arrangement. Should you have any questions, please do not hesitate to contact me directly or reach out to KBI\'s counsel, Dr. Luisa Trautmann, Bergström Hecht Rechtsanwälte, Maximilianstraße 42, 80538 Munich, Germany. We kindly request your written acknowledgment and acceptance by June 23, 2025.

Sincerely, / *Mit freundlichen Grüßen,*

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

Annika Solberg Vice President, Digital Transformation Kessler-Brandt Industrial GmbH Leopoldstraße 155, 80804 Munich, Germany

cc: Dr. Markus Fiedler, Chief Technology Officer, Kessler-Brandt Industrial GmbH cc: Dr. Luisa Trautmann, Bergström Hecht Rechtsanwälte cc: Rajesh Malhotra, Chief Executive Officer, Whitmore Analytics Inc. (courtesy copy)

**ACKNOWLEDGED AND AGREED**

By signing below, the undersigned, on behalf of Whitmore Analytics Inc., acknowledges receipt of this side letter and agrees to the most-favored-customer pricing provision set forth in Section 2 above and to its incorporation into the definitive Technology License Agreement between Kessler-Brandt Industrial GmbH and Whitmore Analytics Inc.

WHITMORE ANALYTICS INC.

By: **\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_**

From: Tom Kendrick <tkendrick@whitmoreanalytics.com>
To: Sarah Yin <syin@whitmoreanalytics.com>
Cc: Rajesh Malhotra <rmalhotra@whitmoreanalytics.com>
Date: Tue, 03 Jun 2025 08:47:00 -0000
Subject: KBI Term Sheet — Initial Reactions and Concerns
Content-Type: text/plain; charset="utf-8"
Content-Transfer-Encoding: quoted-printable
MIME-Version: 1.0

Sarah,

I finished my first full read of the KBI term sheet last night and wanted to =
get my initial reactions to you and Raj while they're fresh. As you know, we =
received the term sheet yesterday (June 2) from Annika Solberg's team =E2=80=
=94 it's dated May 29, 2025, and was prepared by KBI's outside counsel, Bergs=
tr=C3=B6m Hecht Rechtsanw=C3=A4lte (Dr. Luisa Trautmann is the lead partner).=
 The documents =E2=80=94 kbi-whitmore-term-sheet.docx and the kbi-mfc-side-le=
tter.docx =E2=80=94 were circulated to the three of us on Monday, so I'm assu=
ming you've had a chance to at least skim them.

Bottom line up front: I'm excited about this deal. Very excited. But I have s=
erious concerns about three provisions that could do real damage to Whitmore'=
s business if we accept them as-is.

**Why This Deal Matters**

Let me frame the commercial significance first. This would be Whitmore's larg=
est single licensing deal =E2=80=94 by a wide margin. I've run the numbers on=
 first-year revenue:

- License fees: $4,200,000
- SaaS recurring: $2,850,000
- Implementation services: $1,750,000
- Maintenance: $756,000
- **Total first-year: ~$9,600,000**

Against our FY 2024 revenue of $47.3M, that's a flagship deal =E2=80=94 rough=
ly 20% of last year's total. KBI's SmartFactory 2030 initiative is targeting =
43 facilities across 12 European countries with a goal of 40% reduction in un=
planned downtime. Landing KBI as a PredictIQ customer would be the single mos=
t powerful proof point we could have in European heavy manufacturing. This is=
 the reference account that opens the continent.

And the timing matters. Hollcroft Ventures has been asking pointed questions=
 about our path to a Series C round. A deal of this magnitude =E2=80=94 espec=
ially the recurring SaaS component at $2.85M/year =E2=80=94 would materially =
strengthen our growth story. I spoke informally with Hollcroft Point's board obser=
ver last week, and they're "very interested" in the KBI opportunity's contrib=
ution to our revenue trajectory. This deal could be the catalyst for a Series=
 C process later this year or early next.

All of that said, here are my concerns:

**1. Exclusivity / Non-Compete =E2=80=94 This Is My Biggest Issue**

The 3-year exclusivity provision is, in my view, the single most dangerous te=
rm in the sheet. It's not just the 12 named companies on Exhibit B =E2=80=94 =
which themselves include some significant prospects. It's the open-ended defi=
nition that sweeps in *any entity* deriving more than 30% of annual revenue f=
rom automotive or heavy machinery manufacturing in Europe.

I pulled together a quick analysis this morning, and I believe that 30% reven=
ue threshold could capture at least 25 to 30 additional companies beyond the =
12 named ones. Many of these are active prospects in our European pipeline. S=
arah, we currently have late-stage discussions with 3 European automotive/hea=
vy machinery manufacturers for PredictIQ deployments. I won't name them here,=
 but combined potential annual contract value across those 3 is in the range =
of $4-5M in recurring SaaS revenue.

Think about that: our total annual revenue is $47.3M. We'd be locking ourselv=
es out of a significant portion of the European industrial market for three f=
ull years in exchange for one deal =E2=80=94 a big deal, yes, but one that co=
uld actively stunt our growth trajectory and damage the Series C valuation na=
rrative we're trying to build.

My take: the named-company list in Exhibit B might be something we can work w=
ith, though I'd want to negotiate it down. But the open-ended 30% revenue thr=
eshold definition is a non-starter from a go-to-market perspective. I need yo=
u to push hard on narrowing this.

**2. Data Training Restriction =E2=80=94 A Slow-Moving Threat to PredictIQ's =
Core Value**

The second major concern is the clause prohibiting Whitmore from using KBI's =
operational data =E2=80=94 even in anonymized or aggregated form =E2=80=94 to=
 train or improve PredictIQ's general ML models without KBI's prior written c=
onsent.

I want to make sure the strategic significance of this is clear. PredictIQ's =
competitive advantage comes from our ML models continuously improving through=
 exposure to diverse, real-world operational data sets. KBI would be our larg=
est deployment by facility count =E2=80=94 43 facilities generating massive v=
olumes of sensor and operational data across 12 European countries and divers=
e manufacturing environments. Blocking even anonymized use of that data would=
 deprive PredictIQ of critical training inputs that improve prediction accura=
cy for *all* of our customers.

I spoke with the VP of Engineering about this yesterday. The product team has=
 confirmed that data diversity is the single most important factor in model i=
mprovement =E2=80=94 and KBI's data, given the geographic and operational var=
iety, would be uniquely valuable. There is nothing else like it in our curren=
t customer base.

If we accept this restriction, we're essentially allowing our biggest custome=
r to degrade our product for everyone else. Other SaaS/ML companies in our sp=
ace =E2=80=94 and KBI's team surely knows this =E2=80=94 treat anonymized, ag=
gregated data usage as a standard license term. KBI's position here is an out=
lier.

I understand KBI has legitimate data protection interests, especially given G=
DPR and German data protection sensitivities. But there has to be a middle gr=
ound. Anonymization plus aggregation plus a minimum pool size requirement so =
individual KBI data can never be reverse-identified =E2=80=94 something like =
that should address their concerns without gutting our model improvement pipe=
line.

**3. Implementation Payment Structure =E2=80=94 Cash Flow Mismatch**

The implementation services fee of $1,750,000 is spread linearly over the 18-=
month implementation period at approximately $97,222/month. The first signifi=
cant license fee milestone =E2=80=94 $1,680,000 (the 40% installment) =E2=80=
=94 isn't due until Phase 1 completion at month 6 (March 15, 2026, with Phase=
 1 starting September 15, 2025).

I've run the rough math on our first 6 months:

- Signing payment (30% of license): $1,260,000
- Implementation fees (6 =C3=97 $97,222.22): ~$583,333
- **Total first-6-month inflows: ~$1,843,333**

Now consider what we'll be spending. Deploying implementation teams across 43=
 facilities in 12 European countries requires significant upfront investment =
=E2=80=94 international travel, on-site integration specialists, SCADA/MES sy=
stem integration work, customer training personnel. I estimate we'd need to d=
edicate a team of 8-12 engineers and consultants to this deployment, plus tra=
vel and lodging across Europe. My back-of-envelope estimate is that the first=
 6 months could cost us $2.5-3M in implementation costs (loaded salaries + tr=
avel + equipment), creating a potential cash shortfall of $700K to $1.2M befo=
re the Phase 1 milestone payment hits.

Can we restructure the payment schedule? I'd suggest pushing for a larger upf=
ront signing payment =E2=80=94 40-50% instead of 30% =E2=80=94 or acceleratin=
g the implementation fee payments so they're front-loaded rather than linear.=
 This also has implications for revenue recognition treatment =E2=80=94 can y=
ou flag this for Lakeshore Accounting Group LLP when you talk to our auditors?

**Next Steps**

Despite these concerns, I want to be clear: this deal is strategically critic=
al for Whitmore. We should pursue it aggressively =E2=80=94 but not at the co=
st of our core business model or growth trajectory.

I'd recommend we loop in Daniel Cortez and Priya Venkatesh at Ferndale Hale & =
Seward LLP for a thorough review of both the term sheet and the MFC side lett=
er. KBI's requested response deadline is June 23, 2025, and our board meeting=
 is June 18 =E2=80=94 so we need a term sheet analysis ready by mid-June at t=
he latest. That's tight.

Let's get a call on the calendar for Thursday to align before we engage outsi=
de counsel.

Best,
Tom

Tom Kendrick
Vice President, Sales
Whitmore Analytics, Inc.
tkendrick@whitmoreanalytics.com

From: Sarah Yin <syin@whitmoreanalytics.com>
To: Tom Kendrick <tkendrick@whitmoreanalytics.com>
Cc: Rajesh Malhotra <rmalhotra@whitmoreanalytics.com>
Date: Tuesday, June 3, 2025, 11:22 AM PDT
Subject: RE: KBI Term Sheet =E2=80=94 Initial Reactions and Concerns

Tom,

Thank you for the thorough first-pass =E2=80=94 this is exactly the kind of c=
ommercially grounded analysis that helps me prioritize the legal review. I've=
 been going through the term sheet and the separate MFC side letter since yes=
terday afternoon, and I agree with all three of your flagged concerns. I want=
 to confirm and amplify each, and add a few additional items I'll be flagging=
 for outside counsel.

**Exclusivity =E2=80=94 Worse Than You Think**

I share your concern about the exclusivity provision, and I want to add a leg=
al dimension that compounds the commercial problem you've identified. The 30%=
 revenue threshold doesn't just sweep in additional companies =E2=80=94 it cr=
eates an affirmative monitoring obligation for Whitmore. To comply with this =
clause, we would effectively need to determine, before engaging any prospecti=
ve European customer, whether that entity derives 30% or more of its annual r=
evenue from automotive or heavy machinery manufacturing.

We have no reliable way to make that determination for privately held compani=
es, for subsidiaries of diversified conglomerates, or for any entity that doe=
sn't publicly disaggregate its revenue by vertical. This means we face a perp=
etual risk of inadvertent breach =E2=80=94 signing a customer we reasonably b=
elieved fell outside the exclusivity scope, only for KBI to later argue other=
wise. The remedies for breach of the exclusivity clause include termination f=
or cause and liquidated damages, so the downside exposure is significant.

Additionally, and this is something I want to make sure we flag: the exclusiv=
ity period of 3 years is compounded by KBI's expansion right. Under the term =
sheet, KBI can deploy PredictIQ to newly acquired facilities at the same comm=
ercial terms for 5 years post-signing. So KBI locks in favorable economics fo=
r up to 5 years while we're locked out of a major market segment for 3 years.=
 The asymmetry here is stark, and I think it's worth framing it in exactly th=
ose terms when we respond.

**Data Training Restriction =E2=80=94 The Output Data Problem**

Your concerns about the data training restriction are well-taken, and I want =
to flag a related issue that significantly deepens the problem. The term shee=
t's definition of "Output Data" in the Data Rights section defines that term =
to include "model weights, parameters, and training artifacts derived from KB=
I Data." Critically, KBI claims ownership of all Output Data.

This is a separate but closely interrelated issue. The training data restrict=
ion says we can't use KBI data to improve our general models. But the Output =
Data definition goes further =E2=80=94 it suggests that any time PredictIQ pr=
ocesses KBI data, the resulting changes to model state (weights, parameters, =
artifacts) become KBI's property. The interaction of these two provisions cou=
ld effectively mean that PredictIQ's ML models, to the extent they are shaped=
 by KBI data during the ordinary course of providing services, generate IP th=
at belongs to KBI.

I don't think I need to spell out how dangerous that is. This needs very care=
ful analysis from Ferndale Hale & Seward, and I'll be asking Daniel Cortez to =
make it a priority item.

**Implementation Cash Flow =E2=80=94 Confirmed and a Structural Suggestion**

I've confirmed your math on the first-6-month inflows =E2=80=94 approximately=
 $1,843,333.33 against what sounds like a realistic cost estimate of $2.5-3M.=
 The cash flow mismatch is a real operational risk, and I'll want to model th=
is out in detail with the finance team. I'll also flag the revenue recognitio=
n implications for Lakeshore Accounting Group LLP =E2=80=94 the linear paymen=
t structure versus milestone-based delivery creates some questions about ASC =
606 treatment that our auditors should weigh in on early.

One structural suggestion: rather than simply renegotiating the payment perce=
ntages within the current framework, we should consider whether the implement=
ation services agreement should be broken out as a separate Statement of Work=
 with its own milestone-based payment schedule. This would better match cash =
inflows to actual resource expenditure across the phases and would also give =
us cleaner accounting treatment. I'll include this as a recommendation in our=
 counter-proposal framework.

**Additional Items for Outside Counsel**

I'll be sending the term sheet and side letter to Daniel Cortez and Priya Ven=
katesh at Ferndale Hale & Seward LLP with a request for a comprehensive key-te=
rms extraction memo that we can make board-ready for the June 18 meeting. Bey=
ond the three issues you've raised, I want outside counsel to focus on the fo=
llowing =E2=80=94 these are first-impression red flags I'll detail in my inst=
ruction memo to Ferndale Hale:

- **IP ownership provisions:** The derivative works clause and the feedback c=
lause appear to be internally contradictory =E2=80=94 the feedback clause gra=
nts KBI a perpetual license to all "suggestions, enhancement requests, and fe=
edback," which could be read to overlap with derivative works that would othe=
rwise remain Whitmore's IP.
- **Uncapped IP indemnification:** We're being asked to provide uncapped inde=
mnification for third-party IP infringement claims. Given the complexity of t=
he ML/AI patent landscape, this is a significant exposure.
- **Performance warranty with financial penalties:** The 92% prediction accur=
acy threshold tied to financial penalties needs careful scrutiny =E2=80=94 ac=
curacy metrics in predictive maintenance are highly sensitive to data quality=
, sensor calibration, and environmental variables, many of which are outside =
Whitmore's control.
- **Source code escrow release triggers:** The triggers appear to include "ma=
terial breach" without adequate cure periods.
- **Post-termination wind-down license:** The 24-month wind-down license at r=
educed fees could effectively give KBI continued use of PredictIQ well beyond=
 termination.
- **German governing law:** This is a significant issue I want specialist adv=
ice on.

Finally, the MFC side letter from Annika Solberg deserves its own discussion.=
 No carve-outs, no sunset provision, and retroactive application to any prici=
ng concessions Whitmore has offered to other customers since January 1, 2024.=
 It's the most aggressive MFC clause I've seen =E2=80=94 and I've seen quite =
a few.

**Process**

I'm on board for the Thursday call Tom suggested. I'll have a preliminary iss=
ues list organized by priority and risk level ready by then. Raj =E2=80=94 be=
fore I engage Ferndale Hale, I'd appreciate your top-level read on the deal an=
d any strategic parameters you want me to convey to Daniel and Priya regardin=
g our negotiation posture.

Thanks =E2=80=94 Sarah

Sarah Yin
General Counsel
Whitmore Analytics, Inc.
syin@whitmoreanalytics.com

---

From: Rajesh Malhotra <rmalhotra@whitmoreanalytics.com>
To: Sarah Yin <syin@whitmoreanalytics.com>; Tom Kendrick <tkendrick@whitmorea=
nalytics.com>
Date: Tuesday, June 3, 2025, 2:15 PM PDT
Subject: RE: KBI Term Sheet =E2=80=94 Initial Reactions and Concerns

Sarah, Tom =E2=80=94

Thank you both for the quick turnaround on this. I read the term sheet last n=
ight and went through the MFC side letter this morning. I want to give you my=
 strategic framing and set the process so we can move quickly.

**Strategic Framing**

This is the most significant commercial opportunity Whitmore has seen since w=
e founded the company in 2017. Let me put it in context: approximately $9.6M =
in first-year revenue represents roughly 20% of our FY 2024 revenue of $47.3M=
. The recurring SaaS component alone =E2=80=94 $2.85M/year =E2=80=94 would be=
 our largest single SaaS contract by a factor of three. This deal will be the=
 centerpiece of any Series C pitch to Hollcroft Ventures and other investors=
. It validates PredictIQ in precisely the market segment where we need credib=
ility.

But I want to be equally clear about the other side of this: I will not sign =
a deal that compromises Whitmore's core IP or our business model. PredictIQ's=
 value =E2=80=94 and therefore Whitmore's value =E2=80=94 rests on two things=
: our algorithms and the continuous improvement of our ML models through data=
 exposure. Any term that undermines either of those is a dealbreaker, not a n=
egotiation point. We are not going to trade our future for one contract, rega=
rdless of its size.

With that framing, here's where I land on the three issues you've both flagge=
d:

**Exclusivity **

Agreed =E2=80=94 the exclusivity as drafted is too broad and must be narrowed=
 substantially. My position: Whitmore might accept a narrow named-company exc=
lusivity limited to the 12 companies on Exhibit B, for a shorter period of 18=
 months rather than 3 years. But the open-ended 30% revenue definition has to=
 go entirely. The European industrial market is too critical to our growth pl=
an, and Tom is right that the monitoring obligation Sarah described is practi=
cally unworkable. We're not going to accept a provision that turns every pros=
pective European customer engagement into a legal risk assessment exercise.

Tom =E2=80=94 I'll need you to prepare a one-page summary of the European pip=
eline impact of the exclusivity clause for the board deck. Quantify what we'd=
 be giving up: the 3 active prospects, the $4-5M in potential recurring reven=
ue, and the 25-30 additional companies that could fall within the 30% thresho=
ld. The board needs to see those numbers side by side with the KBI deal econo=
mics.

**Data Training Restriction **

Sarah, your analysis of the Output Data definition interaction is exactly wha=
t I was worried about when I read the Data Rights section last night. This is=
 a fundamental threat to PredictIQ's product roadmap.

My position is firm: anonymized, aggregated data usage for model improvement =
is non-negotiable. Whitmore must retain the right to use anonymized and aggre=
gated KBI data to train and improve our general ML models. I'm willing to agr=
ee to strong anonymization standards =E2=80=94 industry-standard techniques, =
contractual commitments, minimum aggregation pool sizes =E2=80=94 and even th=
ird-party verification of the anonymization process if KBI requires that leve=
l of assurance. But a blanket prohibition on using operational data from 43 f=
acilities across 12 countries to improve a product whose entire value proposi=
tion is learning from operational data? That is not something we can accept. =
Full stop.

The Output Data ownership claim also needs to be struck or fundamentally rest=
ructured. Model weights and parameters that result from processing data in th=
e ordinary course of providing services cannot belong to KBI. That would mean=
 every customer interaction makes our own product less ours. Daniel Cortez ne=
eds to flag this prominently.

**Implementation Cash Flow **

I can confirm that Whitmore's current cash position can absorb a short-term m=
ismatch of the magnitude Tom described, but I'd strongly prefer not to fund a=
 customer's implementation out of our operating reserves when the contract it=
self can be structured to avoid it.

Tom =E2=80=94 please model out a revised payment proposal along these lines: =
push for 40% at signing ($1,680,000) and 30% at Phase 1 completion ($1,260,00=
0), with the remaining 30% at Phase 2 go-live ($1,260,000). That reverses the=
 current 30/40/30 split to a 40/30/30 structure that front-loads cash to matc=
h our cost profile. On the implementation side, suggest front-loading the fee=
 payments =E2=80=94 perhaps 40% of the implementation fee payable in the firs=
t 6 months rather than a linear monthly spread. Run the numbers and let's see=
 how that changes the first-6-month cash picture.

Sarah =E2=80=94 please flag the separate SOW structure you suggested. I think=
 that's smart from both a cash management and accounting standpoint.

**Timeline and Process**

Here's how I want this to proceed:

1. **Sarah:** Engage Ferndale Hale & Seward LLP immediately. Send Daniel Corte=
z and Priya Venkatesh the term sheet, the MFC side letter, and a summary of t=
he internal team's concerns as outlined in this email chain. Request a compre=
hensive key-terms extraction memo that is board-ready by **June 16** =E2=80=
=94 two days before the June 18 board meeting. The board will need to approve=

2. **Sarah:** Also flag for Daniel the German governing law issue specificall=
y. I have concerns about our liability exposure under German law =E2=80=94 pa=
rticularly around the uncapped indemnification provisions and the performance=

ashington state law or at least a neutral jurisdiction (England, perhaps, or =
Switzerland).

3. **Tom:** Prepare the one-page European pipeline impact summary for the boa=
rd deck, as noted above.

4. **Thursday call:** Confirmed. Let's use that session to align on our negot=
iation red lines before the Ferndale Hale engagement is fully underway.

Keep in mind the broader timeline: targeted definitive agreement execution is=
 August 15, 2025, with Phase 1 implementation starting September 15, 2025. Th=
ere's some runway, but not much =E2=80=94 and Dr. Markus Fiedler and Annika S=
olberg will expect a substantive response from us promptly after the June 23 =
deadline. We can't afford to be slow here.

This deal could be transformational, but only if the terms don't handcuff us.=
 Let's be aggressive but smart in our response.

result:
The 3-year exclusivity bars Whitmore from licensing PredictIQ — or any "substantially similar" successor product — to any "Direct Competitor" in European automotive and heavy-machinery manufacturing. The definition sweeps in (a) 12 named companies and (b) **any entity deriving more than 30% of revenue** from those sectors across the EEA, UK, and Switzerland. Internal analysis estimates the 30% threshold could capture **25–30 additional companies**, including **three active late-stage European prospects representing \$4–5 million in potential recurring SaaS revenue** — against Whitmore's total FY 2024 revenue of \$47.3M. The obligation **survives termination** and is enforceable by **injunction without a bond or irreparable-harm showing**. The open-ended definition also creates an **unworkable affirmative monitoring obligation**: Whitmore would have to determine, before engaging any European prospect, whether it crosses the 30% threshold — which is not reliably ascertainable for private companies, subsidiaries of diversified conglomerates, or entities that do not disaggregate revenue. *(Sources: Term Sheet §12; emails of T. Kendrick and S. Yin, June 3, 2025.)*

**C-2. Data-training restriction guts PredictIQ's model-improvement pipeline (Section 4.3).**
Whitmore is prohibited from using KBI Data — or data derived from it — to train, tune, or improve PredictIQ's general ML models, to develop/improve third-party products, or to create anonymized/aggregated/de-identified datasets, **without KBI's prior written consent (which may be withheld in KBI's sole discretion)**. KBI's 43-facility, 12-country deployment would be Whitmore's largest and most geographically/operationally diverse data source; the product team has confirmed data diversity is the single most important factor in model improvement. Accepting this restriction would allow KBI to degrade PredictIQ for all other customers and is an outlier position in the SaaS/ML market. *(Sources: Term Sheet §4.3; emails of T. Kendrick and R. Malhotra, June 3, 2025.)*

**C-3. Output Data ownership transfers model state to KBI (Section 4.2).**
"Output Data" is defined to include **model weights, parameters, training artifacts, feature-importance rankings, and model-configuration data derived from KBI Data**, and is declared the **sole and exclusive property of KBI**. Read together with Section 4.3, this could mean that model state shaped by KBI data in the ordinary course of providing services becomes KBI's IP — i.e., every customer interaction makes PredictIQ "less ours." This is a fundamental threat to Whitmore's product roadmap and IP. *(Sources: Term Sheet §4.2; email of S. Yin, June 3, 2025.)*

**C-4. MFC side letter is perpetual, uncabined, retroactive, and auditable (Side Letter).**
Because the license term is perpetual and the wind-down license is perpetual, the MFC has **no sunset**. It contains **no carve-outs** (volume, product mix/bundling, service-level differentials, term-length differentials, government/educational, distressed/acquisition, pilot/POC, or affiliate pricing). It imposes a **retroactive** adjustment and credit obligation (effective as of the date more-favorable terms were first offered to a Subsequent Licensee). It captures PredictIQ **and any successor, derivative, or substantially similar technology**. It grants KBI an **annual audit right** over Whitmore's entire PredictIQ licensing book (with cost-shifting to Whitmore on a ≥5% discrepancy), exposing competitively sensitive pricing to a customer's auditor. The "effective per-facility price" metric is blunt and ignores scope/services/term differences, so a smaller, differently-scoped deployment could trigger MFC parity even where the deals are not truly comparable. KBI treats acceptance as a condition to proceeding. *(Sources: Side Letter §§2–4; email of S. Yin, June 3, 2025.)*

**C-5. Uncapped IP indemnification (Section 9.1).**
Whitmore must defend and indemnify KBI (and its affiliates, officers, directors, employees, and agents) against any third-party IP-infringement claim arising from KBI's authorized use of PredictIQ, **expressly outside any limitation of liability or damages cap**. There is **no carve-out for claims arising from KBI's modifications, combinations, or derivative works**, and no obligation on KBI to suspend use or mitigate. Given the volume and complexity of ML/AI patent litigation, this is significant uncapped exposure. *(Sources: Term Sheet §9.1; emails of S. Yin and R. Malhotra, June 3, 2025.)*

**M-11. Uncapped fee escalations compound over a perpetual term (Sections 5.2, 5.4, 13.1, 13.5).** The SaaS subscription escalates 3% per annum from Year 4 and the Annual Maintenance and Support Fee escalates 4% per annum from Year 2. Because the license term is **perpetual** (§13.1) and the post-termination wind-down license is also **perpetual** and conditioned on continued payment of the Annual Maintenance Fee (§13.5), these escalations **compound indefinitely with no cap or collar**. Over a long horizon this can materially erode the economics (e.g., the maintenance fee, already 18% of the license fee, more than doubles in ~18 years at 4% and is payable forever, including during wind-down). A perpetual SaaS/maintenance term with uncapped escalations is also unusual; SaaS is typically term-based with capped or CPI-based escalations. *(Sources: Term Sheet §§5.2, 5.4, 13.1, 13.5.)*

### Tier 4 — Lower Priority / Missing Provisions (Gaps to Fill)", "old_string": "The default interest rate is high; the "or the maximum rate permitted by applicable law, whichever is less" cap mitigates usury concerns but the headline rate may draw objection and, under German law, may be unenforceable as excessive. *(Source: Term Sheet §5.5.)*

### Tier 4 — Lower Priority / Missing Provisions (Gaps to Fill)"}

result:
### A. Critical Issues — Recommended Red Lines

**1. Exclusivity / non-compete (C-1).** *Management position (R. Malhotra):* Whitmore might accept a **narrow named-company exclusivity limited to the 12 Exhibit B companies, for 18 months (not 3 years)**; the open-ended 30%-revenue definition must be removed entirely. *Our recommendation:* Adopt management's position. In addition: (a) limit the restriction to PredictIQ only (not "any successor or substantially similar product"); (b) make the obligation **non-surviving** on KBI's termination for convenience or for performance failure (it should not survive a KBI walk-away); (c) require KBI to **post a bond / show irreparable harm** for injunctive relief, or remove the injunctive-relief carve-out; (d) add a **mutual** non-solicit limited to Whitmore personnel; and (e) obtain a one-page European pipeline impact summary (T. Kendrick) quantifying the 3 active prospects, the \$4–5M potential recurring revenue, and the 25–30 additional companies, for the board deck and the response to KBI.

**2. Data-training restriction (C-2).** *Management position (R. Malhotra):* Anonymized, aggregated data usage for model improvement is **non-negotiable**; Whitmore must retain the right to use anonymized and aggregated KBI data to train and improve general ML models, subject to strong anonymization standards, minimum aggregation pool sizes, and (if KBI requires) third-party verification of the anonymization process. *Our recommendation:* Adopt management's position as a red line. Redraft Section 4.3 to (a) permit use of **de-identified, aggregated** KBI Data for model improvement subject to defined anonymization standards (e.g., k-anonymity / differential-privacy thresholds) and a minimum pool size; (b) remove KBI's sole-discretion consent right for such aggregated use; and (c) preserve KBI's legitimate restrictions on **re-identification and on use of raw, identifiable** KBI Data.

**3. Output Data ownership (C-3).** *Management position (R. Malhotra):* The Output Data ownership claim must be **struck or fundamentally restructured**; model weights and parameters resulting from processing data in the ordinary course of providing services cannot belong to KBI. *Our recommendation:* Adopt as a red line. Redraft Section 4.2 so that KBI owns **reports, predictions, alerts, and analytics outputs** delivered to it, but **Whitmore retains ownership of model weights, parameters, training artifacts, and model-configuration data** (which are Whitmore IP, optionally licensed to KBI for internal use). Clarify that improvements to PredictIQ's general models remain Whitmore's exclusive property.

**4. MFC side letter (C-4).** *Our recommendation:* Negotiate materially before accepting. Seek: (a) a **defined sunset** (e.g., 3 years from execution, or coterminous with the initial SaaS term rather than the perpetual license/wind-down); (b) **standard carve-outs** — volume/enterprise discounts to larger customers, different product modules/bundles, different service levels, different term lengths, government/educational pricing, distressed/acquisition transactions, pilot/POC pricing, and affiliate pricing; (c) **no retroactivity** (prospective adjustment only, from the date of the more-favorable agreement); (d) a **true comparability test** (same scope, services, term, geography, and product) rather than a blunt per-facility metric; (e) a **narrowed audit right** with a confidentiality/non-use undertaking by the auditor and a defined scope limited to PredictIQ pricing (not Whitmore's entire licensing book); and (f) confirmation of the look-back period (see L-18). If KBI insists on an MFC as a condition, these cabining terms are essential.

Uncapped fee escalations over a perpetual term (M-11).** *Our recommendation:* (a) **Cap or collar the escalations** (e.g., the lesser of 3%/4% or CPI, with a defined aggregate cap), or convert to a fixed schedule; (b) reconsider the **perpetual SaaS/maintenance term** — consider a defined initial term (e.g., 5 years) with renewal at Whitmore's then-current rates, rather than a perpetual term with uncapped escalations; and (c) ensure any wind-down maintenance fee is **fixed or capped**, not subject to the 4% annual escalation in perpetuity.

**26. Missing provisions (L-1 through L-17).** *Our recommendation:* Add, in the definitive agreement: a data-processing agreement and security standards (L-1); EU SCCs and a transfer impact assessment (L-2); a KBI warranty and indemnity as to lawfulness of KBI Data (L-3); IP-indemnity carve-outs for KBI modifications/derivative works/combinations (L-4); OSS disclosure and compliance (L-5); export-control compliance (L-6); anti-corruption/sanctions representations (L-7); force majeure (L-8); insurance requirements (L-9); a mutual no-hire/no-solicit (L-10); reference/customer-logo rights for Whitmore (L-11); tax/withholding mechanics and treaty/W-8 forms (L-12); transition-assistance services (L-13); a Whitmore license-compliance audit right (L-14); a complete survival clause including warranties and dispute resolution (L-15); mutual injunctive-relief rights (L-16); and a confirmed FX allocation (L-17). Reconcile the internal-document discrepancies (L-18) before responding."}

Communicate to KBI that Whitmore is enthusiastic about the partnership and the SmartFactory 2030 initiative, but that several terms as drafted are incompatible with Whitmore's core IP and business model and require restructuring. Frame the exclusivity asymmetry (KBI locks in favorable economics for up to 5 years via the expansion right while Whitmore is locked out of a market segment for 3 years) explicitly in the response.

\newpage

## IX. Items Requiring Further Diligence / Open Questions

1. **Executed Exhibits A and B.** Confirm the final PredictIQ description (Exhibit A) and the Direct Competitor list (Exhibit B), and obtain the separate technical specification document referenced in Exhibit A (minimum system requirements).
2. **German-law specialist review.** Obtain focused advice on governing law, the uncapped indemnification, and the performance-warranty penalties under German law (including any unenforceability or reasonableness limits under German civil law).
3. **MFC look-back date.** Confirm whether KBI intends a January 1, 2024 look-back (referenced internally) or only the retroactivity to the date more-favorable terms were first offered to a Subsequent Licensee (as the side letter text provides). Obtain any earlier draft of the side letter.
4. **Wind-down license terms.** Confirm whether the definitive agreement will adopt the term sheet's perpetual wind-down license at the full maintenance fee or the internally referenced 24-month/reduced-fee structure.
5. **Counsel-name discrepancy.** Reconcile "Fenwick Hale & Seward LLP" (side letter) vs. "Ferndale Hale & Seward LLP" (engagement emails) and ensure the correct firm name is used in the definitive agreement.
6. **Open-source and third-party components.** Inventory OSS and third-party licensed components in PredictIQ to scope the escrow exclusion (H-5) and the OSS disclosure (L-5).
7. **Data-protection specifics.** Obtain KBI's standard data-processing terms and confirm the sub-processor list, breach-notification timelines, and the cross-border transfer mechanism.

The general cap is the greater of 2× fees paid in the preceding 12 months or **\$15,000,000**. In early years, 2× annual fees is roughly \$5.7M–\$7.6M, so the \$15M floor will govern — a cap several multiples of annual contract value. Confidentiality breaches (Section 15) are also carved out of the cap (Section 10.3(B)), which is significant given the breadth of the KBI Data restrictions. *(Source: Term Sheet §10.)*

**H-5. Source-code escrow deposit is broad and may exceed Whitmore's rights (Section 11.1).**
The deposit requires "complete source code … including all modules, libraries, and dependencies," plus build tools, compilers, scripts, and complete technical documentation, updated annually and on each major release. This may include **third-party licensed components** (e.g., open-source or commercially licensed libraries) that Whitmore may not have the right to escrow, creating a potential breach of Whitmore's own upstream licenses. *(Source: Term Sheet §11.1.)*

Internal estimates put first-6-month inflows at ~\$1.843M (signing \$1.26M + 6 months implementation ~\$583K) against implementation costs of \$2.5–3M (8–12 engineers/consultants plus European travel/lodging/equipment), a **shortfall of \$700K–\$1.2M** before the Phase 1 milestone payment. The linear implementation billing versus milestone-based delivery also raises ASC 606 revenue-recognition questions for the auditors (Lakeshore Accounting Group LLP). *(Sources: Term Sheet §§5.1, 5.3; email of T. Kendrick, June 3, 2025.)*

**H-2. Performance warranty with financial penalties and a termination trigger (Sections 8.2–8.3, 13.4).**
